refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve end-to-end teleoperation communication latency with p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions. Refinement clarifies that this is an end-to-end requirement spanning sensor acquisition, transmission, processing, and actuation feedback.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency from operator input (e.g., joystick command) through network transmission to equipment response (e.g., actuator movement) and feedback return to operator display. Conduct testing under agreed network profiles (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss). Collect p95 latency statistics across representative operational scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Sensor-to-network latency shall be minimized to support end-to-end budget.
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transmission latency shall not exceed allocated budget under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.3: Processing and actuation response latency shall be minimized to support end-to-end budget.
FR-NGHE-008.4: Operator feedback (visual, haptic) latency shall be compatible with safe teleoperation.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement edge computing with local sensor processing and predictive control algorithms to reduce round-trip latency dependency.
DP-B: Deploy dedicated low-latency network infrastructure (5G/private network) with QoS prioritization for teleoperation traffic.
DP-C: Architect teleoperation system with distributed processing (edge + cloud) and latency-aware scheduling to meet p95 budget.
DP-D: Implement hardware-accelerated signal processing and direct sensor-to-actuator feedback loops with minimal software overhead.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Architect teleoperation system with distributed processing (edge + cloud) and latency-aware scheduling to meet p95 budget.

potential_sources: DP-C couples teleoperation architecture (Teleoperation module) with network infrastructure (Network Link interface) and safety validation (Safety Services module).
Distributed processing introduces complexity in state synchronization and failover logic, requiring careful interface design.
Latency-aware scheduling depends on accurate network condition monitoring and dynamic load balancing.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is selected because it balances operational flexibility (supports variable network conditions via adaptive scheduling) with safety assurance (edge processing enables local safety checks). It avoids over-dependence on network infrastructure alone (DP-B) while maintaining operator control responsiveness. Coupling is manageable through well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Edge devices (vehicle-mounted compute) have sufficient processing capacity for local control algorithms.
Assumption: Network conditions are predictable enough to enable latency-aware scheduling.
Risk: Synchronization delays between edge and cloud processing could exceed latency budget if not carefully managed.
Risk: Failover from cloud to edge (or vice versa) could introduce transient latency spikes.
Risk: Operator training required to understand latency-dependent control behavior in edge vs. cloud modes.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) latency budget allocation across sensor acquisition, network transmission, edge processing, cloud processing, and actuation; (2) switching criteria and latency impact for edge/cloud mode transitions; (3) safety validation requirements for edge-executed control algorithms.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the teleoperation control architecture, including operator interface, command transmission, edge/cloud processing coordination, feedback rendering, and latency monitoring. Responsible for implementing distributed processing logic and latency-aware scheduling to meet p95 ≤ 150 ms requirement.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from remote operator station. Tasking interface carries command semantics and timing metadata.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits sensor data (position, orientation, load, environmental) and system state to operator display and cloud processing. Includes latency-critical feedback for operator situational awareness.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services (e.g., geofence violations, load limits). Transmits safety-relevant telemetry and edge-computed safety status. Bidirectional for safety validation and override coordination.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts underlying network transport (5G, private network, satellite). Teleoperation module monitors Network Link latency/jitter and adapts processing distribution (edge vs. cloud) accordingly.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of teleoperation latency architecture. All distributed processing logic, scheduling, and latency monitoring are core to this module's responsibility. Minimal diffusion: only Network Link interface abstraction is external.

Vehicle module owns actuators and local sensors but does not own teleoperation control logic or operator interface. Assigning latency architecture to Vehicle would scatter teleoperation concerns across vehicle firmware and remote operator station, violating cohesion.

Safety Services owns safety validation and constraints but not teleoperation control. Latency architecture is orthogonal to safety services; mixing them would conflate safety logic with performance optimization.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but too coarse-grained for this DP. Assigning to NGHE Solution would defer architectural decisions and diffuse responsibility across multiple sub-modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module contains the distributed processing architecture, latency-aware scheduling logic, and operator interface coordination. Vehicle module provides sensor/actuator interfaces via Telemetry and Tasking. Safety Services provides safety constraints via SafetyAlert. Network Link abstracts transport latency.
containment_risks: Risk: If edge processing (on Vehicle) grows complex, boundary between Teleoperation and Vehicle control logic may blur. Mitigation: Define clear API contract for edge-executed algorithms (e.g., local safety checks, predictive control) owned by Teleoperation but executed on Vehicle hardware.

Risk: Latency monitoring and adaptive scheduling require real-time feedback from Network Link. If Network Link interface is poorly defined, Teleoperation cannot accurately assess network conditions. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link provides latency/jitter metrics with sufficient granularity and timeliness.

Risk: Safety-critical decisions (e.g., emergency stop) may need to bypass Teleoperation latency optimization. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface must support priority-based message handling independent of latency scheduling.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between Teleoperation (remote control logic, scheduling) and Vehicle (edge compute execution). Define a formal contract specifying which algorithms execute on edge vs. cloud, and how Teleoperation module coordinates this distribution. Ensure Network Link interface provides latency metrics with <10 ms granularity for adaptive scheduling.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (distributed processing with latency-aware scheduling) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 (p95 ≤ 150 ms teleoperation latency). The DP provides a logical solution path: edge processing reduces round-trip dependency, cloud processing handles complex tasks, and adaptive scheduling optimizes latency under variable network conditions. No independence violation detected; the DP is necessary and sufficient for the FR.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must coordinate with Vehicle (edge compute), Safety Services (constraints), and Network Link (transport). Four external interfaces create moderate coupling, but each interface has clear semantics and direction.

Latency-aware scheduling logic in Teleoperation depends on accurate Network Link metrics. If Network Link interface is unreliable or delayed, scheduling decisions will be suboptimal. Mitigation: Establish SLA for Network Link latency reporting (<10 ms).

Edge processing algorithms (owned by Teleoperation, executed on Vehicle) require careful versioning and deployment coordination. Mitigation: Define formal API and versioning strategy for edge algorithm distribution.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score. The DP is well-localized within Teleoperation with clear external dependencies. No significant diffusion detected. Ownership is unambiguous: Teleoperation team owns the distributed processing architecture and latency optimization logic.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Test harness can inject operator commands, measure round-trip latency through Teleoperation module, and collect p95 statistics. Network Link interface can be mocked to simulate various latency/jitter profiles.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving p95 ≤ 150 ms. Sub-allocations to Vehicle (sensor latency), Safety Services (constraint validation latency), and Network Link (transport latency) can be derived from end-to-end budget. Verification plan can be traced to Teleoperation module ownership.

recommended_revisions: Establish formal latency budget allocation: sensor acquisition (<20 ms), network transmission (<80 ms), edge processing (<20 ms), cloud processing (<20 ms), actuation feedback (<10 ms). Total: 150 ms p95.
Define Network Link interface SLA: latency metrics reported with <10 ms granularity, jitter estimates updated every 100 ms, packet loss statistics available for adaptive scheduling.
Specify edge algorithm versioning and deployment strategy to ensure Teleoperation can push algorithm updates to Vehicle without service interruption.
Document failover behavior: if cloud processing exceeds latency budget, Teleoperation must automatically switch to edge-only mode with graceful degradation of functionality.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs addressing sensor latency, network transmission, processing, and feedback. DP-C (distributed processing) is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module. However, the sub-FRs require further decomposition to establish concrete design parameters for each latency component. Proceeding to the next level will refine the latency budget allocation and identify specific technologies (e.g., edge compute platform, scheduling algorithm, network protocol) needed to realize DP-C.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Sensor-to-network latency shall be minimized to support end-to-end budget.
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transmission latency shall not exceed allocated budget under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.3: Processing and actuation response latency shall be minimized to support end-to-end budget.
FR-NGHE-008.4: Operator feedback (visual, haptic) latency shall be compatible with safe teleoperation.

assumptions: Edge devices (vehicle-mounted compute) have sufficient processing capacity for local control algorithms.
Network conditions are predictable enough to enable latency-aware scheduling.
Operator training is available to support edge/cloud mode transitions.
Safety Services module can provide constraints with <5 ms latency impact.
Network Link interface can report latency metrics with <10 ms granularity.
open_risks: Synchronization delays between edge and cloud processing could exceed latency budget if not carefully managed.
Failover from cloud to edge (or vice versa) could introduce transient latency spikes.
Edge algorithm versioning and deployment could introduce service interruptions.
Network Link interface may not provide sufficient latency visibility for adaptive scheduling.
Safety-critical decisions may conflict with latency optimization objectives.
evidence_needed: Latency budget allocation across sensor, network, processing, and actuation components.
Edge compute platform specifications and processing capacity analysis.
Network Link interface definition and latency reporting SLA.
Adaptive scheduling algorithm design and simulation results.
Failover strategy and transient latency impact analysis.
Safety validation approach for edge-executed control algorithms.
Operator training plan and human factors analysis for latency-dependent control.
